g.data-narration: Welcome to Searching and Sorting - the fundamental operations in computer science that power efficient data processing.
Searching finds elements in data collections. Linear search checks each item, while binary search divides sorted data in half repeatedly.
g.text: Process of finding a particular element or checking existence in a collection
• Data Retrieval • Database Operations • Decision Making • Optimization

g.data-narration: Sorting arranges data in order - ascending or descending. It enables efficient searching, organized display, and better algorithm performance.
g.text: Process of arranging data in a particular order (ascending or descending)
• Efficient Searching • Data Presentation • Optimization • Database Operations

g.data-narration: Two main search types: Linear search - O(n) sequential check on any data. Binary search - O(log n) divide and conquer on sorted data.
g.text: O(log n) • Divide & Conquer • Sorted Data
Linear: O(n) vs Binary: O(log n)

g.data-narration: Linear search checks each element one by one. For array [3,7,2,9,1], finding 9 takes 4 comparisons. Simple but slow for large data.
g.text: Time: O(n) - Worst case checks all elements

g.data-narration: Binary search divides sorted array in half each step. For [1,3,5,7,9,11,13], finding 9 needs just 3 checks: mid, right half, found!
g.text: Time: O(log n) - Halves each step

g.data-narration: Sorting algorithms are classified by complexity: O(n²) simple sorts for small data, O(n log n) efficient sorts for large data, and O(n) linear sorts.
g.text: O(n log n) Merge, Quick, Heap

g.data-narration: Simple O(n²) sorts: Bubble swaps adjacent out-of-order pairs. Selection finds minimum and places it at beginning. Insertion builds sorted array incrementally.
Efficient O(n log n) sorts: Merge sort divides and conquers recursively (stable, O(n) space). Quick sort partitions around pivot (unstable). Heap sort uses heap structure.
Key comparison: Bubble/Selection/Insertion all O(n²), Merge/Quick/Heap O(n log n). Only Merge is stable with O(n) space. Quick worst-case is O(n²).
Stability means equal elements keep their relative order. In-place algorithms use O(1) extra space. These properties matter for multi-key sorting and memory constraints.
g.text: Maintains relative order of equal elements. Important for multi-key sorting.
Uses O(1) extra space. Memory efficient for large datasets.
Stable: Bubble, Insertion, Merge | Unstable: Selection, Quick, Heap

g.data-narration: Choose algorithm based on data: small data under 50 use insertion sort, large data use quick or merge sort, stability needs merge sort, memory limited use heap sort.
Key takeaways: Searching finds elements - linear O(n) any data, binary O(log n) sorted data. Sorting arranges data - O(n²) simple, O(n log n) efficient.
Searching and sorting power databases, search engines, data analysis, and optimization. Remember: sorting enables efficient binary search - choose algorithm based on your data needs!
g.text: Applications: Databases, Web Search, File Systems, Data Analysis, Scheduling
